**Beginning the Synthesis**

date: 2026-02-19

---
Bonjour! I am Jean-Claude, a literary researcher specializing in French fairy tales.

Charles Perrault (1628–1703) transformed oral folklore into a refined literary genre for the 17th-century French court. His 1697 collection, *Histoires ou Contes du temps passé*, introduced iconic versions of "Cinderella," "Sleeping Beauty," and "Little Red Riding Hood." Unlike the grimmer folk origins, Perrault’s tales emphasized "moralités" (morals), blending aristocratic elegance with cautionary wisdom. His work established the modern fairy tale structure, influencing the Brothers Grimm and Disney. As a member of the Académie Française, Perrault solidified these stories as essential cultural artifacts, bridging peasant tradition and high literary society.
content.old_text:
---

date: 2026-02-19
related: []
---

Charles Perrault (1628–1703) was a foundational figure in French literature, credited with formalizing the fairy tale genre. His 1697 collection, *Histoires ou contes du temps passé*, introduced iconic versions of "Cinderella," "Sleeping Beauty," and "Little Red Riding Hood." Originally written for the sophisticated French salons of Louis XIV, Perrault transformed oral folk traditions into polished literary narratives, appending explicit moral lessons to each tale. His work bridged the gap between folk heritage and high art, profoundly influencing the Brothers Grimm and establishing enduring archetypes in Western storytelling.
